questions.prompt: If you can only do five things before launching an AI feature, which set covers the highest-risk failures?

choices.text: Choose GPT-5, write a great system prompt, add a logo, write marketing copy, set up analytics.
Eval suite (≥30 cases), per-request logging, daily spend cap, feature flag with one-click disable, approval gates on irreversible tool calls. These five catch ~90% of production AI disasters.
Fine-tune the model, switch to reasoning, run on Batch API, hire a head of AI, buy a vector DB.

questions.explanation: These five address the most common ways AI launches fail: silent quality regressions (eval), unfixable bugs (logging), cost surprises (cap), unrollbackable mistakes (feature flag), and irreversible actions gone wrong (approval gates). Everything else on the production checklist is hardening — these are the floor.

questions.prompt: You launch a feature and a misconfigured loop drives the bill to $11,000 overnight. What process control would have caught this within hours instead of overnight?

choices.text: A bigger model would have refused to run the loop.

A daily spend cap on the API key plus cost alerts at threshold breaches. Both stop runaway costs in minutes-to-hours rather than the next morning. Combined with a feature flag, you can disable the offending feature instantly while you debug.
Hiring a finance person to watch the bill in real time.
Nothing — it's just the cost of doing AI.

questions.explanation: API providers offer hard spend caps and rate alerts for exactly this. Set them before launch, not after a six-figure surprise. Combine with circuit breakers in code (if X requests fail or cost exceeds Y, stop calling) and feature flags for instant rollback. The pattern is: cap, alert, flag, disable.
